questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of binary search on a sorted array of size n? Derive the recurrence relation and give the closed-form solution.
keyPoints: Worst-case time complexity is O(log n)
Assumes infinite precision arithmetic and no overflow

questions.question: Given a sorted array with duplicate values, describe how binary search can be modified to locate the first occurrence of a target key. Provide the key idea and a short pseudocode snippet.
keyPoints: Continue searching the left half when arr[mid] == target
Maintain a 'result' variable to store the latest valid index

questions.question: Implement binary search in C++ (or Java/Python) to find both the first and last occurrence of a given key in a sorted array that may contain duplicates. Include handling of edge cases (key not present, array empty, single element) and analyze the time and space complexities of your solution.
keyPoints: Two helper functions: one for first occurrence, one for last occurrence
Use while (low <= high) with mid = low + (high - low) / 2 to avoid overflow
Edge cases: return [-1, -1] when key not found; handle empty array and single-element array
Overall time O(log n) for each search; space O(1) auxiliary

questions.question: Consider the scenario where binary search is applied to a rotated sorted array (e.g., [4,5,6,7,0,1,2]). Design an algorithm to search for a target value in O(log n) time. Explain the logic, provide pseudocode, and discuss the reasoning behind choosing the appropriate half based on the comparison of the middle element with the boundaries.
keyPoints: Identify the sorted half by comparing arr[mid] with arr[low] and arr[high]
Determine if the target lies within the sorted half; adjust low or high accordingly
Recurrence still yields O(log n) because each iteration discards half of the array
Handles edge cases: rotation by 0 (normal sorted array) and may require extra handling for duplicate values
